coredata.dc:title: One-step synthesis of ultrabright amphiphilic carbon dots for rapid and precise tracking lipid droplets dynamics in biosystems
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Dong
Zhang, Yusheng
Wu, Kai
Min, Hanyun
Wei, Dan
Sun, Jing
Yang, Huaqing
Fan, Hongsong
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent probes enabling precisely labeling lipid droplets (LDs) in complex systems are highly desirable in life science for studying LDs-related physiological processes and metabolic diseases. However, most of the current LDs fluorophores fail to achieve rapid wash-free LDs labeling, especially in vivo labeling due to their strong hydrophobicity and poor water solubility. We report here one-step synthesis of highly efficient carbon dots (CDs) that feature robust solvatochromic emission, high quantum yield (QY) up to 76.35% in oil, good water solubility and lipophilicity, thus allowing to stain LDs in a bright and selective manner. Detailed characterizations reveal the presence of a well-defined molecule, 2-dimethylamino-5-fluorobenzimidazole in a large amount in CDs. Its D-π-A structure and dimethylamino-induced spatial torsion configuration and extended π-electron conjugation account for solvatochromic emission with high QY. Notably, the CDs can image LDs with many advanced merits (high brightness, ultrafast staining within 10 s, wash-free, excellent LDs specificity, good biocompatibility) and have been successfully applied to monitor cellular LDs dynamics. Moreover, the CDs for the first time allow in situ labeling of LDs and epidermal cell membranes simultaneously in live zebrafish. This work expands the diversity for optical properties and applications of CDs, facilitating the design of new LDs-targeting CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Polarity
Ultrahigh quantum efficiency
Lipid droplets
Fluorescence imaging
In vivo imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Monitoring the dynamics of LDs during starvation
Synthesis and optical characterization of the synthesized CDs
Structure characterizations of the synthesized CDs
Proposed photoluminescent mechanism of the synthesized CDs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51973132 , 52003178 ) and the Applied Basic Research Programs of Sichuan Province ( 2021YJ0173 , 2021YFH0011 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photographs of LD-CDs in various solvents under UV light excitation. (b) Corresponding chromaticity diagram. Normalized absorption spectra (c) and emission spectra (d) of LD-CDs in various solvents. (e) Plot of the emission maximum of LD-CDs in various solvents versus ET(30). (f) Time-resolved PL spectra of LD-CDs in different solvents under 390 nm excitation. (g) Absorption and PL spectra of LD-CDs in oil and PL spectra in PBS buffer. (h) Photographs of LD-CDs and G-CDs in the mixed solvents of PBS and oil under day light and UV light. (i) PL intensity (green) and absolute PLQYs (red) of LD-CDs with different concentrations in oil under 420 nm excitation (inset: photographs under day light and UV light). (j) Fluorescent spectra of LD-CDs powder under various excitation (inset: photographs of LD-CDs powder under daylight and UV light). (k) Absolute PLQYs of LD-CDs and G-CDs in various solvents. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) TEM image of LD-CDs (inset: particle size distribution). (b) HRTEM image of LD-CDs. (c) AFM image of LD-CDs (inset: the height profile along the line in c). (d) FTIR spectra of LD-CDs and G-CDs. (e) XPS spectra and high-resolution (f) C 1s, (g) N 1s, (h) O 1s and (i) F1s spectra of LD-CDs and G-CDs.

(a) TGA spectra of the two CDs. (b) ESI-MS spectra of the two CDs obtained in positive ion mode. 1H-NMR spectra of (c) LD-CDs recorded in CDCl3 and (d) G-CDs recorder in DMSO. (e) Possible formation mechanism of the two CDs. (f) The top and side view of the structures, (g) HOMO and LUMO orbitals and (h) molecular electrostatic potential maps of the two molecules (Top: 1-H-5-fluorobenzimidazole; Down: 2- dimethylamino-5-fluorobenzimidazole).

(a) Confocal images of HepG2 cell stained with LD-CDs for different times. (b) Corresponding fluorescence intensities. (c) Colocalization images of HepG2 cells between LD-CDs and BODIPY 493/503. The 3D reconstructed image of HepG2 cells (d) stained with LD-CDs and (e) co-stained with LD-CDs and nuclear probe RedDot1. (f) Normalized excitation spectrum of LD-CDs in oil and in situ emission spectrum of LD-CDs in cellular LDs. (g) Multicolor images of HepG2 cells co-stained with RedDot1, LD-CDs and Mito-tracker Red. (h) Merging of g. (i) Enlarge of h. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) CLSM of HeLa cells incubated with LD-CDs and BODIPY 493/503 before treatments, after fixation and permeabilization treatment. (b) CLSM of HeLa cells incubated in HBSS for 0 h (control), 4 h and 8 h, followed by staining with LD-CDs and MitoTracker Red, respectively. (c) CLSM of LD-CDs- and MitoTracker Red-stained HeLa cells treated with 10 mM H2O2 for 0 min, 10 min and 20 min. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(a) CLSM of zebrafish at different developmental stages stained with LD-CDs. (b–c) CLSM of zebrafish surface stained with LD-CDs and the enlarged view with single-cell resolution. (d) The pattern of zebrafish. (e–f) The enlarged view of the zebrafish yolk sac.
Current LDs probe: (a) Organic fluorophores. (b) AIE luminogens. (c) CDs-based LDs probe in present work. (d) Schematic illustration of the preparation of LDs-specific CDs.

head.ce:title: One-step synthesis of ultrabright amphiphilic carbon dots for rapid and precise tracking lipid droplets dynamics in biosystems

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Engineering Research Center for Biomaterials, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610064, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610065, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Sichuan University, Chengdu, 610065, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent probes enabling precisely labeling lipid droplets (LDs) in complex systems are highly desirable in life science for studying LDs-related physiological processes and metabolic diseases. However, most of the current LDs fluorophores fail to achieve rapid wash-free LDs labeling, especially in vivo labeling due to their strong hydrophobicity and poor water solubility. We report here one-step synthesis of highly efficient carbon dots (CDs) that feature robust solvatochromic emission, high quantum yield (QY) up to 76.35% in oil, good water solubility and lipophilicity, thus allowing to stain LDs in a bright and selective manner. Detailed characterizations reveal the presence of a well-defined molecule, 2-dimethylamino-5-fluorobenzimidazole in a large amount in CDs. Its D-π-A structure and dimethylamino-induced spatial torsion configuration and extended π-electron conjugation account for solvatochromic emission with high QY. Notably, the CDs can image LDs with many advanced merits (high brightness, ultrafast staining within 10 s, wash-free, excellent LDs specificity, good biocompatibility) and have been successfully applied to monitor cellular LDs dynamics. Moreover, the CDs for the first time allow in situ labeling of LDs and epidermal cell membranes simultaneously in live zebrafish. This work expands the diversity for optical properties and applications of CDs, facilitating the design of new LDs-targeting CDs.

ce:section.ce:para: Lipid droplets (LDs), as highly dynamic lipid-rich organelles, are generally composed of a hydrophobic core of neutral lipids (mainly cholesteryl ester and triacylglycerol) and a phospholipid monomembrane (Collot et al., 2018). Despite the basic function of regulating lipid storage, LDs have attracted considerable attention in recent years as they are found to be closely associated with diverse physiological processes, such as anti-microbial capability, membrane trafficking, protein storage, inflammation (Chen et al., 2021; Wang et al., 2021a). More importantly, the abnormality of LDs is a critical biomarker for many diseases, such as obesity, cancer, fatty liver, atherosclerosis and Alzheimer's disease (Hu et al., 2019; Tian et al., 2021; Zheng et al., 2018). Although the mechanisms that how LDs can directly affect these biological processes are ambiguous, it is well-accepted that these functions are closely associated with the dynamics of LDs. Hence, visualizing and tracking LDs is of prior importance in early diagnosis of related disease as well as basic study of their biological functions (Liu et al., 2021b).

Fluorescence imaging techniques enable direct visualization of lipids in vitro and in vivo by offering various key advantages including high sensitivity, fast response, high resolution and noninvasive manner (Li et al., 2020; Lichtman and Conchello, 2005). Although increasing efforts have been devoted to developing highly efficient LDs-specific fluorophores (Collot et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2017), most of them suffer from poor water solubility due to their high lipophilicity (Wang et al., 2021b), and their easy precipitation in aqueous media usually leads to reduced fluorescence in aggregation state due to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), long staining times and complicated post-cleaning procedures, thus causing incomplete and inaccurate imaging of LDs and vibration of cellular microenvironment, further resulting in low labeling efficiency and biased conclusions. More importantly, due to these drawbacks, few of current LDs fluorophores are available for in vivo LDs labeling in living systems (Wang et al., 2021b). Therefore, it is imperative to develop a novel water-soluble fluorophore to realize rapid, simple and wash-free LDs labeling both in vitro and in vivo.

Carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging fluorescent nanomaterial bearing bright emission, high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) and photostability, good water solubility and simple preparation (Li et al., 2021), have demonstrated great potential to serve as fluorescence probes in bioimaging (Chung et al., 2021). CDs capable of target various organelles, such as lysosome, mitochondria, and nucleolus have been widely reported (E et al., 2021; Han et al., 2019), whereas LDs-targeting CDs are quite rare. Generally, most LDs-specific fluorophores possess proper amphiphilicity and obvious solvatochromism (Zheng et al., 2018), fortunately, these critical features for LDs-specific labeling are feasible for CDs. However, the “black box” reaction of CDs and the unclear relationship between the precise chemical structure and optical properties as well as binding abilities greatly hinder the rational design of CDs with desired properties (E et al., 2021).

Herein, we report the discovery of highly efficient carbon dots (CDs)-based LDs-specific probe by one-step solvothermal treatment of 1,2-diamino-4-fluorobenzene and thiourea in N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) (Scheme 1 ). The obtained CDs exhibit remarkable solvatochromic emission, high PLQY up to 76.35% in oil media, large Stokes shift up to 155 nm and favorable anti-ACQ solid-state fluorescence (SSF). Systematic characterizations reveal that the CDs mainly consist of carbonized core and a large amount of molecular fluorophore, 2-dimethylamino-5-fluorobenzimidazole, whose typical electron-donating (D)-π-bridge(s)-electron-accepting (A) (D-π-A) feature coupled with dimethylamino-induced spatial torsion configuration and extended π-electron conjugation account for solvatochromic emission with high QY. Specifically, the CDs possess excellent amphiphilicity and can stain intracellular LDs within 10 s in a wash-free manner and allow high-quality and long-term LDs imaging. Moreover, we firstly clearly reveal LDs in yolk sac and epidermal cell membranes in live zebrafish using the CDs-based probe.
All chemicals, reagents and materials are presented in Supplementary Information (SI), section S1.1.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) micrographs were measured on FEI Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope. Atom force microscopy (AFM) were recorded using Bruker AFM Dimension Icon. UV–Vis absorption was characterized by a U-2910 UV/VIS spectrophotometer. Fluorescence emission and excitation spectra were collected using Horiba Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Absolute quantum yield measurements were performed with a calibrated integrating sphere on Horiba Fluorolog-3 spectrophotometer. Fluorescence lifetimes was performed using a Horiba Fluorolog-3 corrected single photon-counting (TCSPC) system. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed on a Nicolet 6700 infrared spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data was carried out on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD spectrometer. High-resolution electrospray ionization-mass spectrometry (ESI-MS) spectra were obtained on a Thermo Scientific Q Exactive Plus. Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy was done on Bruker BioSpin AV11-600 MHz. The decomposition temperature thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was measured on a PerkinElmer TGA 7 thermogravimetric analyzer under a nitrogen atmosphere from room temperature to 800 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1.

The LD-CDs were synthesized by one-pot solvothermal method. Typically, 1,2-diamino-4-fluorobenzene (1 mmol) and thiourea (1 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL DMF. Then the mixture was transferred to a 100 mL poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. After the reactor was cooled down to room temperature automatically, the obtained solution was firstly puriﬁed via 0.22 μm filter to remove large particles. Then, the crude products were purified via silica column chromatography using dichloromethane as the eluent. After collecting purified solution and removing the solvent through rotary evaporator, green powders can be collected. For the preparation of G-CDs, the reaction conditions were the same as those of LD-CDs except lacking thiourea. During purification process, ethyl acetate was used as the eluent.
Details of cell culture and MTT assays can be found in SI, section S1.2. and S1.3.

HepG2 cells (1 × 105) were seeded in glass bottom cell culture dishes and incubated in 1 mL Dulbecco's Modiﬁed Eagle Medium (DMEM) with 10% fetal bovine serum and 1% antibiotics at 37 °C in a humidified atmosphere of 5% CO2 for 24 h. Subsequently, the cells were stained with 4 μM BODIPY 493/503 for 10 min and then washed with 1 mL PBS for three times. After that, the cells were stained with 1 mL LD-CDs PBS solution (10 μg mL−1) for a few minutes and then used for imaging directly. Zeiss LSM880 confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) was used to measure the cellular fluorescence images. Excitation: 405 nm for LD-CDs; 488 nm for BODIPY 493/503. The co-localization experiments of LD-CDs with Red DotTM1, Mitro Tracker Red, Lyso-Tracker Red were similar to that of BODIPY493/503. RedDotTM1: λex = 633 nm; Mitro Tracker Red: 2 μM, λex = 543 nm; Lyso Tracker Red: 2 μM, λex = 543 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Monitoring the dynamics of LDs during starvation
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cells were incubated in Hank's balanced salt solution (HBSS) for different times (0–8 h). After that, the treated cells were stained with Mito Tracker Red (2 μM) for 30 min and LD-CDs (10 μg mL−1) for 5 min. Next, the cells were washed with PBS for three times. Cells were immediately subjected to cell imaging by Zeiss LSM880 CLSM for LD-CDs (λex = 405 nm) and Mito Tracker Red (λex = 543 nm). The details of monitoring the dynamics of LDs during oxidative stress can be found in SI, section S1.4.

Zebrafish embryos were purchased from Shanghai FishBio Co., Ltd. Zebrafish embryos at 4 hpf (hours post fertilization) were placed in culture dishes and incubated in methylene blue-rich deionized water at 28 °C. For confocal imaging, zebrafish embryos at 4 hpf were firstly stained with LD-CDs (10 μg mL−1) for 10 min. The stained zebrafish embryos were immediately observed under Zeiss LSM880 CLSM. Subsequently, the stained 4 hpf zebrafish embryos were further incubated into different developing stages. Corresponding confocal imaging at different developing stages was recorded under Zeiss LSM880 CLSM.

The imaging process of zebrafish larval (5 dpf, days post fertilization) was similar to that of zebrafish embryos. During imaging process, the zebrafish larval was anesthetized by 0.003% tricaine methanesulfonate (MS222). All animal experiments were approved by the ethics committee of Sichuan University in accordance with the protocol.

Full geometry optimizations were operated to locate all of the stationary points, using the M06-2X density functional theory (DFT) method with 6–311++G(d, p) basis set for all atoms, namely, M06-2X/6–311++G(d,p). The time-dependent DFT (TDDFT) method was used to calculate the excited states. The solvent effect was considered with the polarizable continuum model (PCM). All calculations in this work were performed using Gaussian 09 program package.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and optical characterization of the synthesized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The LDs-specific CDs (LD-CDs) were facilely synthesized by solvothermally heating 1,2-diamino-4-fluorobenzene and thiourea in N, N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) at 200 °C for 8 h (Scheme 1), followed by strict purification through silica gel column chromatography (Fig. S1a). As control, by heating 1,2-diamino-4-fluorobenzene in DMF at 200 °C for 8 h, we also obtained solvatochromic CDs (named G-CDs), but with inferior PLQY compared to LD-CDs (Fig. S1b). Notably, another control experiment of heating thiourea in DMF was also conducted and only blue fluorescent species were generated (Fig. S1c). As a result, 1,2-diamino-4-fluorobenzene is the basis for solvatochromic of LD-CDs while thiourea is critical for their high quantum efficiency.

The photophysical properties of LD-CDs were firstly investigated. Apart from the typical excitation-independent characteristics (Fig. S2), LD-CDs display remarkable positive solvatochromic property on their emission with fluorescence finely tuned from blue to orange as the solvent polarity increases (Fig. 1 a–b), the corresponding maximum emission gradually shifts from 475 nm to 575 nm (Fig. 1d), along with a pronounced decrease in PL intensity (Fig. S3). However, unlike the largely red-shifted fluorescence, the absorption maximum in various solvents merely varies from 397 nm to 420 nm (Fig. 1c). Additionally, a good linear relationship with a correlation coefficient of R2 = 0.991 between the maximum PL and the solvent polarity parameter (ET(30)) is observed (Fig. 1e). All these results suggest that LD-CDs present typical solvatochromic emission, which is frequently attributed to intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) effect. To gain more insight into the PL mechanism of LD-CDs, time-resolved PL spectra in different solvents were measured. The PL decay curves of LD-CDs in solutions can be well fitted by a monoexponential decay (Fig. 1f; Table S1), revealing that the radiative decay process is extremely pure with a single emissive center (Yuan et al., 2018). As a results, we propose that a molecular state with typical D-π-A structure may serve as the single emissive center of LD-CDs. The absolute PLQY of LD-CDs in solutions was measured to be as high as 75.19%, moreover, a large Stokes shift up to 155 nm was achieved (Table S2), which are favorable for high signal-to-background (SB) contrast of fluorescent imaging. Notably, G-CDs also show solvatochromic emission with fluorescence tuned from green to orange as solvent polarity increases (Figs. S4a–e), which is similar to LD-CDs, suggesting their PL centers are also dominated by D-π-A structures. However, the PLQYs of G-CDs in various solutions range from 3.43 to 20.48%, which are much lower than those of LD-CDs (Fig. 1k; Tables S2 and S3).

Subsequently, the photophysical properties of LD-CDs in oil media-glyceryl trioleate (the main component of LDs) were measured. As expected, LD-CDs display excellent solubility in oil with relatively narrow absorption band centered at around 410 nm (Fig. 1g). Moreover, LD-CDs display ultrabright green fluorescence in oil with an impressive fluorescent enhancement up to 100-fold in comparison with that in aqueous solution (Fig. 1g). Remarkably, the absolute QYs of LD-CDs in oil media and PBS are 76.35% and 1.77%, respectively (Figs. S5a–b), thereby a pronounced SB contrast in a mixed oil/PBS environment can be clearly observed for LD-CDs (Fig. 1h). In comparison, G-CDs only show limited fluorescent enhancement of 5-fold from oil to aqueous solution (Fig. S4f), and the QYs of G-CDs in oil and PBS are determined to be 10.28% and 3.43% (Figs. S5c and d), respectively, resulting in an inferior SB contrast (Fig. 1h). Additionally, the fluorescent properties of LD-CDs in the high concentration state were studied since continuous accumulation of a fluorophore in the hydrophobic core of LDs usually leads to reduced fluorescence owning to ACQ effect (Zhang et al., 2020). The results show that LD-CDs still emit bright fluorescence efficiently even at a high concentration of 1 mg mL−1 (Fig. 1i). Moreover, under 365 nm UV irradiation, LD-CDs display unique self-quenching-resistant SSF (Fig. 1j). Thus, efficient anti-ACQ behavior can be achieved. Additionally, similar to LD-CDs powder, G-CDs powders also present weak yellow-green SSF (Fig. S6). Taken together, LD-CDs show potential to serve as highly efficient LDs-specific probe using fluorescent microscopy techniques.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure characterizations of the synthesized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology, diameter and crystallographic characteristics of the prepared CDs were measured by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, LD-CDs display well dispersed dot-shape nanoparticles with average diameters of about 2.2 nm. The HRTEM image illustrates that most of LD-CDs possess clear lattice fringes of 0.21 nm (Fig. 2b and Fig. S7), corresponding to the d spacings of graphite (100) facet, demonstrating their crystalline carbon structure (Holá et al., 2017). The AFM image further reveals that LD-CDs are monodispersed with heights of approximately 2–3 nm. These results reveal that LD-CDs are nearly spherical nanoparticles with ultrasmall sizes.

Subsequently, the chemical compositions and surface functional groups were measured by FTIR and XPS. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2d) of LD-CDs shows the stretching absorption bands of –OH and –NH2 groups between 3000–3500 cm−1, the stretching vibration of methyl at 2919 cm−1 and 2852 cm−1, the characteristic absorption peaks of CO, CN and CC bonds in the range of 1500–1750 cm−1. Besides, the absorption peaks at 1431 cm−1 and 1147 cm−1 indicate the presence of C–N and C–F bonds (Jiang et al., 2020), respectively. The XPS data further confirm the results of FTIR. The full XPS spectrum presented in Fig. 2e shows that LD-CDs consist of C, O, N, S and F elements. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of the C 1s band indicates the presence of four main peaks at 284.8, 285.4, 286.3, and 288.8 eV, attributed to C–C/CC, C–N, C–O and C–F, respectively (Fig. 2f). The XPS N 1s spectrum (Fig. 2 g) can be deconvoluted into two peaks at 399.1 and 400.2 eV, respectively, corresponding to pyridinic N and pyrrolic N. The XPS O 1s spectrum shows two fitting peaks at 532.2 and 533.8 eV for CO and C–O bands, respectively (Fig. 2h). Besides, there is only one fitting peak at binding energy of 687.2 eV for the high-resolution F 1s spectrum, assigned to covalent C–F bond (Fig. 2i) (Jiang et al., 2020; Liu et al., 2021a). The XPS S 2p spectrum contains three peaks at 164.9 eV for S–C, 165.2 eV for H–S and 166.4 eV for S–S (Fig. S8). Interestingly, the detailed characterizations of G-CDs by means of TEM, FTIR and XPS show highly comparable morphologies and chemical structures with LD-CDs (Fig. S9; Fig. 2d–i), which lay the foundation for their similar solvatochromic emission.

Taking the unique photophysical behaviors and pure emissive centers of the two CDs into account, we try to assign a precise chemical structure to comprehend their underlying photoluminescence principle (Vallan et al., 2018). TGA results show a large amount of less thermostable chemical moieties and highly thermostable structures in the two CDs, which should be attributed to the surface molecular fluorophores and polyaromatic structures of carbon cores (Fig. 3 a), respectively (Liu et al., 2020a). ESI-MS spectra were then tested and expectantly, two main molecular fractions with mass of m/z = 179 and m/z = 136 are observed for LD-CDs and G-CDs, respectively (Fig. 3b; Figs. S10–11), which correspond to the molecular fluorophores insides the two CDs. NMR spectra were conducted to shed light on the accurate chemical structure of the two molecules. The 1H-NMR and 13C-NMR spectra of LD-CDs show several dominant peaks in the range of 7–8 ppm and 100–160 ppm (Fig. 3c and Fig. S12), respectively, corresponding to aromatic structure (Wang et al., 2020b; Yang et al., 2019). Besides, the 1H-NMR and 13C-NMR spectra display two dominant peaks at 3.10 and 40.81 ppm, respectively, assigned to methyl group (Niu et al., 2018). In the 19F-NMR spectrum, obvious signals appear in the range from −111 to −119 ppm (Fig. S13), attributed to the aromatic fluorine. The above data suggest the tentative structure of 2-dimethylamino-5-fluorobenzimidazole with formula as C9H10N3F for the molecular fluorophore of LD-CDs (Fig. 3e). The inferred molecular structure is further verified by 13C-NMR distortionless enhancement by polarization transfer (DEPT) spectra (Figs. S14–15). It should be noted that the C content of LD-CDs (76.47%) is higher than that of the deduced molecule (60.33%), which should be ascribed to the formation of carbonized core in LD-CDs. Thus, LD-CDs are unambiguously identified as “carbon dots”, consisting of a carbonized core and large number of homogeneous surface molecular fluorophore (Soni et al., 2021). By the same way, we also reveal that the structure of G-CDs resembles that of LD-CDs and the molecular fluorophore of which is defined as 1-H-5-fluorobenzimidazole with formula as C7H5N2F (Fig. 3d and e). Based on these results, a possible formation mechanism of these CDs is proposed. As shown in Fig. 3e, since the precursors, 1,2-diamino-4-fluorobenzene and DMF, contain amino and aldehyde group, respectively, Schiff base reaction occurs during solvothermal treatment, which can further generate benzimidazole structure after undergoing dehydrogenation. Subsequently, during the processes of self-assembly and carbonization, the initially formed benzimidazole intermediate gradually lose dimethylamino group without thiourea, giving rise to G-CDs. Whereas with the existence of thiourea, the formed benzimidazole intermediate is more stable and their dimethylamino group keeps constant as reaction proceeds, yielding highly efficient LD-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Proposed photoluminescent mechanism of the synthesized CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The D-π-A feature of the two deduced benzimidazole structures should be the key reason for solvatochromism emission of the two CDs. In order to further confirm above hypothesis, the photophysical properties of the precursor 1,2-diamino-4-fluorobenzene with D-π-A feature were measured and the results clearly show solvatochromic emission for the precursor, with fluorescence range overlapping well with those of the two CDs (Fig. S16). Therefore, the solvatochromic emissions of the two CDs is identified from the D-π-A structural units. Besides, the totally different PLQYs between the two CDs arouse intense interest due to their role facilitating the comprehension towards the origin of high PLQY. Compared to G-CDs, LD-CDs give rise to dimethylamino in the structure. Theoretical calculation was conducted to explore the influence of dimethylamino group. The optimized molecular geometry clearly reveals that 1-H-5-fluorobenzimidazole is a planar molecule, while the 2-dimethylamino-5-fluorobenzimidazole presents a torsional configuration between the dimethylamino group and the skeleton of benzimidazole (Fig. 3f). To the best of our knowledge, planar molecules always show ACQ effect in solid state due to π-π stacking activating the nonradiation transition (Zheng et al., 2018). Therefore, G-CDs display weak fluorescence in solid state. However, once torsional structure is formed, the spatial effect of the structure increases significantly, which can effectively reduce energy loss generated by molecular π-π stacking (Niu et al., 2018). Consequently, LD-CDs present bright fluorescence in solid state. Moreover, as a strong electron donor, the highest occupied molecular orbitals (HOMO) and the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals (LUMO) and the computed electrostatic potential (ESP) maps demonstrate that the introduction of dimethylamino can increases the density distribution of electronic clouds, extending π-electron conjugation (Fig. 3g–h), which can efficiently enhance fluorescence. It should be noted that the theoretical calculations are based on the two monomers, thus the absolute values are not directly comparable, however, relative comparison and trends enable qualitatively explanations for the observed findings (Holá et al., 2017; Vallan et al., 2018).

Inspired by the superior photophysical properties, we further examined the imaging capability of LD-CDs. Firstly, anti-interference ability of LD-CDs was tested using different potential interferences including pH, various relevant analytes and viscosity. The results demonstrate that LD-CDs show negligible fluorescence responses after exposed to these interfering agents (Fig. S17), implying high stability of LD-CDs in a complex biological system. Next, after incubating with LD-CDs, bright green fluorescence with spherical morphology is observed in HepG2 cells (Fig. S18). During the staining process, a wash-free manner was found available. Under the wash-free procedure, we explored the influence of staining time on the imaging effect and surprisingly, LD-CDs can stain the LDs in cells within only 10 s and no significant change was observed as the staining time increased to 20 min (Fig. 4 a–b). Additionally, the effect of LDs concentrations on staining efficiency were studied. Due to the high QY of LD-CDs, a low concentration of 10 μg mL−1 was found sufficient for LDs imaging (Fig. S19). The cytotoxicity of LD-CDs was further tested and the results indicate that LD-CDs possess good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity (Fig. S20). In order to verify the exact distribution of LD-CDs in cells, co-localization experiment using BODIPY 493/503 was performed, and firstly, there is no cross-talk between BODIPY 493/503 and LD-CDs (Fig. S21). Then, the co-stained experiments demonstrate that the green fluorescence of LD-CDs is mainly distributed on LDs (Fig. 4c), with the Pearson's correlation coefficient of 0.86 (Fig. S22), suggesting superior LDs specificity for LD-CDs. Besides, co-localization experiments of LD-CDs with three typical cellular markers demonstrate that LD-CDs are unable to target other subcellular organelles (Fig. S23). Next, the LDs-specific imaging capability of LD-CDs in different cells were studied and the results indicate that LD-CDs can efficiently light up LDs in different cells (Fig. S24), indicating the universal LDs targeting ability of LD-CDs. The superior LDs-targeting performance of LD-CDs should be closely associated with their unique chemical structure. LDs are of high lipophilicity. Similarly, LD-CDs possess abundant lipophilic CH3 groups on their surface, endowing LD-CDs with high lipophilicity, which should be a critical reason for their specific staining ability. On the other hand, due to the strong solvatochromic emission, LD-CDs can emit strong fluorescence in low polarity LDs, resulting in high contrast LDs imaging in living cells under confocal microscope.

3D imaging with confocal microscopy is generally achieved by reconstruction of Z-stack images, during this process, high brightness and photostability of the fluorophore are indispensable. Herein, due to the high brightness and good photostability (Fig. S25), LD-CDs enable high-quality 3D images of LDs (Fig. 4d). Furthermore, spatial distribution of LDs and their inter-relations with the nucleus are also clearly revealed (Fig. 4e). In addition, the excitation and in situ emission spectrum of LD-CDs reveal that LD-CDs have narrow excitation and emission bandwidths (full width at half-maximum (fwhm) of 50 nm) and only the commonly available 405 nm laser can efficiently excite LD-CDs (Fig. 4f). Therefore, LD-CDs might be efficient to serve as a green emissive LDs marker in multicolor imaging. To achieve this aim, HepG2 cells were stained with (1) RedDot1, a far-red nucleus marker excited using 633 nm laser; (2) LD-CDs, a green LDs probe excited using 405 nm laser and (3) MitoTracker Red, a red mitochondria marker excited using 543 nm laser. As shown in Fig. 4g, no crosstalk is observed among different organelles and high-quality multicolor imaging is successfully obtained (Fig. 4h–i).

Furthermore, the universal LDs staining abilities of LD-CDs in living, fixed and permeabilized cells were tested since in many immunostaining experiments, the stained living cells are usually required to be fixed and permeabilized for further studies. After fixation or permeabilization treatments, both the CDs-stained LDs and BODIPY 493/503-labeled LDs still present bright fluorescence as those before the treatments (Fig. 5 a), demonstrating the universal LDs staining ability of LD-CDs in living, fixed, and permeabilized cells. Subsequently, LD-CDs were further applied to monitor the dynamic movement of LDs. Firstly, we studied the metabolism of LDs during autophagy process induced by cell starvation. HeLa cells were incubated in serum-free HBSS medium for several hours to cause starvation and then co-stained with LD-CDs and Mito-Tracker Red. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, as the starvation time prolongs, a significantly increased intracellular distribution of LDs is observed, revealing the generation of LDs during starvation. Moreover, LDs with green fluorescence are found to gradually accumulate on the mitochondria with red emission, revealing that LDs can move from cytoplasm to mitochondria during starvation. This impressive behavior of LDs agrees well with recent study of starvation-induced “lipophagy” (Nguyen et al., 2017). Next, we further use LD-CDs to real-time track the dynamic changes between LDs and mitochondria during cell apoptosis induced by H2O2-generated ROS. As shown in Fig. 5c, HeLa cells were firstly co-stained with LD-CDs and Mito-Tracker Red, then 10 mM of H2O2 was added to induce cell apoptosis. Initially, LDs and mitochondria show distinguishable green and red fluorescence. As time goes on, the LDs show stable green fluorescence while a noticeable variation with gradually vanished red fluorescence of mitochondria is clearly observed. Meanwhile, some dot-shape red fluorescence appears, which overlaps well with the green fluorescence of LDs in the overlay images. The recorder results are in consistent with recent study that the LDs can protect the cells from oxidation by fusing the lipid-soluble conjugates escaped from oxidative stress-induced mitochondria collapse (Wang et al., 2020a). These results indicate the potential of LD-CDs for real-time tracking the dynamic changes LDs.

In vivo imaging capability of LD-CDs was further studied. Zebrafish is chosen as the in vivo model due to its simple operation, high fecundity, cost-effectiveness, and especially optical transparency, allowing easily to visualize organs, tissues and some subcellular organelles (Liu et al., 2020b). As shown in Fig. 6 a, after incubating with LD-CDs for 5 min, bright green fluorescence could be clearly observed and selectively focused on the lipid-rich yolk sac of zebrafish embryos. As time went on, the zebrafish embryos displayed outstanding tolerance to LD-CDs and developed normally without any sign of lethality and delay of hatching as the control group, indicating no toxic effect of LD-CDs on zebrafish embryos. More importantly, bright green fluorescence could be observed to retain in yolk sac region even after 48 h post staining, and no fluorescence diffused to newly generated trunk and tail region, revealing that LD-CDs have high affinity and long retention time for lipid-rich tissues.

In situ visualizing the LDs and lipid-rich structures in live zebrafish with rapid, simple, and in particular wash-free staining procedure is big challenge. Herein, LD-CDs have demonstrated capable of imaging zebrafish rapidly within 5 min in a wash-free manner, the pattern of zebrafish is clearly visible and a strong green fluorescence is observed and accumulated in yolk sac (Fig. 6d). Upon zooming in, a typical LDs staining pattern could be clearly visualized (Fig. 6e–f). Interestingly, by magnifying zebrafish surface, honeycomb pattern is clearly revealed (Fig. 6b–c), such pattern corresponds to the epidermal cell membranes (Shi et al., 2020). Remarkably, the boundary of every single cell is clear and distinct (Fig. S26). The epidermal cell membranes imaging capability may be due to the good water solubility of LD-CDs, making them easily to penetrate the hydrophilic mucus layer on zebrafish surface (Jia et al., 2019).

In conclusion, for the first time, we successfully develop a novel strategy for the synthesis of highly efficient amphiphilic LDs-specific CDs with outstanding optical properties of pronounced solvatochromic property, favorable anti-ACQ SSF, large Stokes shift, high PLQY (76.35%) in oil and good water solubility. Detailed characterizations identify that LD-CDs mainly consist of a molecular fluorophore, 2-dimethylamino-5-fluorobenzimidazole, whose D-π-A structure coupled with dimethylamino-induced spatial effect and extended π-electron conjugation is responsible for solvatochromic emission with high QY. Notably, the CDs not only allow rapid and wash-free LDs imaging of various cells in vitro, but also achieve simple and noninvasive in situ labelling of LDs of live zebrafish. More intriguingly, the CDs also enable rapid and wash-free in vivo epidermal cell membranes imaging of live zebrafish, thus the prepared CDs may serve as not only highly efficient LDs-specific probe, but also a potential in vivo epidermal cell membranes marker. This work efficiently expands the diversity for optical property and application of CDs, inspiring the development of lipid-specific fluorophores for in vivo applications.

Dong Gao: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yusheng Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Kai Wu: Data curation. Hanyun Min: Software. Dan Wei: Formal analysis. Jing Sun: Formal analysis. Huaqing Yang: Software. Hongsong Fan: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51973132, 52003178) and the Applied Basic Research Programs of Sichuan Province (2021YJ0173, 2021YFH0011).
